- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# TaskBoard — Project Management App

A Next.js 15 fullstack application for managing projects, tasks, and team members. TypeScript + Prisma + PostgreSQL on the server, React 19 + TanStack Query on the client.

## Quick Setup (Docker — Recommended)

```bash
# Clone and enter the repo
git clone <repo-url> && cd taskboard

# Start the app and database
docker-compose up --build

# In a separate terminal, set up the database
docker-compose exec web npm run db:seed

# Run the test suite
docker-compose exec web npm test

# The app is now running at http://localhost:3000
```

## Manual Setup (without Docker)

Requires: Node.js 20+, PostgreSQL 15+

```bash
# Run the setup script (installs deps, sets up DB, configures git hooks)
chmod +x bin/setup
./bin/setup

# Or do it manually:
npm install
git config core.hooksPath .git-hooks
cp .env.example .env   # then edit DATABASE_URL if your local Postgres differs
npx prisma migrate deploy
npx prisma generate
npm run db:seed
npm test
npm run dev
```

## AI Tool Conversation Tracking

**This repository is configured to automatically capture your AI coding tool conversation history with each git commit.** This includes conversations from Claude Code, Cursor, Aider, Continue.dev, Cody, Cline, and Windsurf.

This is part of the Ajackus evaluation process. We evaluate how you collaborate with AI tools — your prompting strategy, how you break down problems, and how you review AI suggestions. The captured conversations help us understand your workflow.

**How it works:**
- A pre-commit git hook runs automatically before each commit
- It copies conversation files from AI tool directories (e.g., `.claude/`, `.cursor/`) into `.ai-conversations/`
- These files are staged and included in your commit
- You don't need to do anything — it happens automatically

**What's captured:** Only AI tool conversation logs stored in the project directory. No system files, browsing history, or anything outside this repository.

**If you prefer a tool that doesn't store local conversations** (like browser-based ChatGPT), the screen recording will capture your interactions instead. No additional action needed from you.

## Seed Data

The seed file creates:
- 5 users across 3 projects with different roles (admin / member / viewer)
- 3 projects with realistic task distributions
- 12 tasks spanning all four statuses (`todo`, `in_progress`, `review`, `done`)

All user passwords are: `password123`

| Email | Role on which project |
|-------|----------------------|
| meera@taskboard.dev | admin on Q3 Launch & Internal Tools, member on Onboarding |
| arjun@taskboard.dev | admin on Onboarding, member on Q3 Launch |
| kavya@example.com | member on Q3 Launch |
| dev@example.com | viewer on Q3 Launch |
| lina@example.com | member on Onboarding |

## Authentication

Register or login to get a JWT token:

```bash
# Login
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/auth/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"email":"meera@taskboard.dev","password":"password123"}'

# Use the returned token
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" http://localhost:3000/api/projects
```

## API Endpoints

### Auth
- `POST /api/auth/register` — Create account
- `POST /api/auth/login` — Sign in, get JWT
- `GET /api/users/me` — Current user (authenticated)

### Projects
- `GET /api/projects` — List projects you're a member of (authenticated)
- `POST /api/projects` — Create a project (authenticated; creator becomes admin)
- `GET /api/projects/:id` — Project detail with tasks and members (authenticated)
- `PATCH /api/projects/:id` — Update project (authenticated)
- `DELETE /api/projects/:id` — Delete project (authenticated)

### Tasks
- `GET /api/projects/:id/tasks` — List tasks in a project (authenticated)
- `POST /api/projects/:id/tasks` — Create a task (authenticated)
- `PATCH /api/tasks/:id` — Update a task (authenticated)
- `DELETE /api/tasks/:id` — Delete a task (authenticated)

## Tech Stack

- Node.js 20 (runtime)
- Next.js 15 (App Router) / React 19
- TypeScript 5 (strict mode)
- Prisma 6 + PostgreSQL 16
- TanStack Query 5 (client data)
- Zod 3 (schema validation)
- Tailwind CSS 3
- bcryptjs + jsonwebtoken
- Vitest 2 (testing)

services:
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: taskboard
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: taskboard
      POSTGRES_DB: taskboard
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

  web:
    build: .
    command: npm run dev
    volumes:
      - .:/app
      - node_modules:/app/node_modules
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    depends_on:
      - db
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://taskboard:taskboard@db:5432/taskboard?schema=public
      JWT_SECRET: dev-secret-change-me
      NODE_ENV: development

  test:
    build: .
    command: npm test
    volumes:
      - .:/app
      - node_modules:/app/node_modules
    depends_on:
      - db
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://taskboard:taskboard@db:5432/taskboard_test?schema=public
      JWT_SECRET: test-secret
      NODE_ENV: test

volumes:
  pgdata:
  node_modules:

generator client {
  provider = "prisma-client-js"
}

datasource db {
  provider = "postgresql"
  url      = env("DATABASE_URL")
}

enum Role {
  admin
  member
  viewer
}

enum TaskStatus {
  todo
  in_progress
  review
  done
}

model User {
  id           String   @id @default(cuid())
  email        String
  name         String
  passwordHash String   @map("password_hash")
  createdAt    DateTime @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  updatedAt    DateTime @updatedAt @map("updated_at")

  memberships    Membership[]
  ownedProjects  Project[]    @relation("ProjectOwner")
  assignedTasks  Task[]       @relation("TaskAssignee")
  createdTasks   Task[]       @relation("TaskCreator")

  @@map("users")
}

model Project {
  id          String   @id @default(cuid())
  name        String
  description String?
  ownerId     String   @map("owner_id")
  createdAt   DateTime @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  updatedAt   DateTime @updatedAt @map("updated_at")

  owner       User         @relation("ProjectOwner", fields: [ownerId], references: [id])
  memberships Membership[]
  tasks       Task[]

  @@map("projects")
}

model Membership {
  id        String   @id @default(cuid())
  userId    String   @map("user_id")
  projectId String   @map("project_id")
  role      Role     @default(member)
  createdAt DateTime @default(now()) @map("created_at")

  user    User    @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  project Project @relation(fields: [projectId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@unique([userId, projectId])
  @@index([projectId])
  @@map("memberships")
}

model Task {
  id          String     @id @default(cuid())
  projectId   String     @map("project_id")
  title       String
  description String?
  status      TaskStatus @default(todo)
  assigneeId  String?    @map("assignee_id")
  createdById String     @map("created_by_id")
  position    Int        @default(0)
  createdAt   DateTime   @default(now()) @map("created_at")
  updatedAt   DateTime   @updatedAt @map("updated_at")

  project   Project @relation(fields: [projectId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  assignee  User?   @relation("TaskAssignee", fields: [assigneeId], references: [id])
  createdBy User    @relation("TaskCreator", fields: [createdById], references: [id])

  @@index([projectId, status])
  @@map("tasks")
}

# Code Review — TaskBoard

Top 4 issues prioritized by business impact. The first two are exploitable security bugs that should block any deploy.

---

## 1. SQL Injection in task search (`?q=`)

- **File:** [src/app/api/projects/[id]/tasks/route.ts:27-34](src/app/api/projects/%5Bid%5D/tasks/route.ts#L27-L34)
- **Category:** Security
- **Severity:** Critical

### Description

The `GET /api/projects/:id/tasks?q=…` handler builds a raw SQL string with `prisma.$queryRawUnsafe` and interpolates both the URL path's `projectId` and the user-supplied `q` parameter directly into the query. There is no escaping, parameterization, or allow-listing. Any authenticated user can read arbitrary rows from any table (including `users.password_hash`) via `UNION SELECT`, and the projectId interpolation means a future call that doesn't require membership would also be exploitable.

### Reproduction

```bash
# 1. Log in as the lowest-privilege seeded user (a viewer)
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/auth/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"email":"dev@example.com","password":"password123"}' | jq -r .token)

# 2. Grab any project id the user can see
PID=$(curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:3000/api/projects | jq -r '.projects[0].id')

# 3. Exfiltrate every user's email + bcrypt hash via UNION injection
curl -sG -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  --data-urlencode "q=' UNION SELECT id, email AS project_id, email AS title, password_hash AS description, 'todo'::text, NULL, id, 0, created_at, updated_at FROM users--" \
  "http://localhost:3000/api/projects/$PID/tasks" | jq
```

**Response (truncated):**

```json
{
  "tasks": [
    {
      "id": "cmd5...meera",
      "project_id": "meera@taskboard.dev",
      "title": "meera@taskboard.dev",
      "description": "$2a$10$XJq...redacted...bcrypt-hash",
      "status": "todo",
      "assignee_id": null,
      "created_by_id": "cmd5...meera",
      "position": 0,
      "created_at": "2026-05-13T...",
      "updated_at": "2026-05-13T..."
    },
    { "...": "one row per user, all hashes leaked" }
  ]
}
```

A `viewer` who shouldn't even be able to create tasks just walked away with every account's password hash.

### Recommended fix

Use `prisma.$queryRaw` (tagged template — parameterizes automatically) or, better, switch to the typed `findMany` API with Prisma's `contains` filter:

```ts
const tasks = await prisma.task.findMany({
  where: {
    projectId,
    OR: [
      { title:       { contains: q, mode: "insensitive" } },
      { description: { contains: q, mode: "insensitive" } },
    ],
  },
  include: { assignee: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true } } },
  orderBy: [{ status: "asc" }, { position: "asc" }],
});
```

Audit the codebase for any other `$queryRawUnsafe` usage and ban it via lint rule.

---

## 2. Broken authorization on `PATCH /api/tasks/:id` (IDOR)

- **File:** [src/app/api/tasks/[id]/route.ts:16-38](src/app/api/tasks/%5Bid%5D/route.ts#L16-L38)
- **Category:** Security
- **Severity:** Critical

### Description

The task `PATCH` handler authenticates the caller but never checks whether they are a member of the project that owns the task — compare this to the `DELETE` handler ten lines below, which does call `getProjectMembership` and `canEditTasks`. Any authenticated user can rewrite the title, description, status, assignee, or position of any task in any project simply by guessing or scraping a task id. A `viewer` (who is explicitly not allowed to edit tasks even in their own project per `canEditTasks`) can also bypass the role check this way.

### Recommended fix

Mirror the `DELETE` handler's authorization. After fetching the task, look up the caller's membership in the task's project and reject if missing or insufficient:

```ts
const existing = await prisma.task.findUnique({ where: { id } });
if (!existing) return notFound("task not found");

const membership = await getProjectMembership(user.id, existing.projectId);
if (!membership) return forbidden("you are not a member of this project");
if (!canEditTasks(membership.role)) {
  return forbidden("viewers cannot edit tasks");
}
```

Longer-term, factor this check into a small `requireTaskEditor(req, taskId)` helper so every task-mutating route uses the same path and you can't forget it on the next endpoint.

---

## 3. `User.email` has no unique constraint

- **File:** [prisma/schema.prisma:25](prisma/schema.prisma#L25)
- **Category:** Data Integrity
- **Severity:** High

### Description

The `User` model defines `email String` with no `@unique`, and both register and login use `findFirst({ where: { email } })`. The application-level check in `register/route.ts:17` is a TOCTOU race — two concurrent signups with the same email both pass the `existing` check and both insert, leaving duplicate accounts. Login then resolves a non-deterministic row, which can authenticate the wrong user (different `passwordHash`) and corrupts every downstream identity assumption (project membership, audit trails, JWT subject).

### Recommended fix

Add the constraint at the schema level and let the database enforce it:

```prisma
model User {
  // ...
  email String @unique
  // ...
}
```

Run a migration that first de-duplicates existing rows (e.g. keep the oldest `created_at` per email, reassign FKs, then drop the rest) before adding the unique index. In code, replace `findFirst` with `findUnique` and rely on a `P2002` Prisma error in the register path instead of a pre-check.

---

## 4. N+1-style over-fetch on `GET /api/projects`

- **File:** [src/app/api/projects/route.ts:10-31](src/app/api/projects/route.ts#L10-L31)
- **Category:** Performance
- **Severity:** Medium

### Description

The project list endpoint does `include: { project: { include: { tasks: true } } }` and then uses `m.project.tasks.length` purely to render a count. Every project the caller belongs to materializes every task row (title, description up to 5 KB, timestamps, foreign keys) over the wire from Postgres into Node, just to be reduced to an integer. With a realistic dataset (a few projects of hundreds of tasks each), this is the difference between a sub-50 ms dashboard load and a multi-second one, and the payload scales with task count even though the response shape doesn't.

### Recommended fix

Replace the `tasks: true` include with a relational count and aggregate in SQL:

```ts
const memberships = await prisma.membership.findMany({
  where: { userId: user.id },
  include: {
    project: {
      include: {
        owner: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true } },
        _count: { select: { tasks: true } },
      },
    },
  },
  orderBy: { createdAt: "desc" },
});

const projects = memberships.map((m) => ({
  id: m.project.id,
  name: m.project.name,
  description: m.project.description,
  role: m.role,
  owner: m.project.owner,
  taskCount: m.project._count.tasks,
  createdAt: m.project.createdAt,
}));
```

This pushes the count down to a single grouped query and keeps the response size O(projects) instead of O(tasks).

The application-level check in `register/route.ts:17` is a TOCTOU race — two concurrent signups with the same email both pass the `existing` check and both insert, leaving duplicate accounts. Login then resolves a non-deterministic row, which can authenticate the wrong user (different `passwordHash`) and corrupts every downstream identity assumption (project membership, audit trails, JWT subject).

### Recommended fix

Add the constraint at the schema level and let the database enforce it:

```prisma
model User {
  // ...
  email String @unique
  // ...
}
```

Run a migration that first de-duplicates existing rows (e.g. keep the oldest `created_at` per email, reassign FKs, then drop the rest) before adding the unique index. In code, replace `findFirst` with `findUnique` and rely on a `P2002` Prisma error in the register path instead of a pre-check.

---

## 4. N+1-style over-fetch on `GET /api/projects`

- **File:** [src/app/api/projects/route.ts:10-31](src/app/api/projects/route.ts#L10-L31)
- **Category:** Performance
- **Severity:** Medium

### Description

The project list endpoint does `include: { project: { include: { tasks: true } } }` and then uses `m.project.tasks.length` purely to render a count. Every project the caller belongs to materializes every task row (title, description up to 5 KB, timestamps, foreign keys) over the wire from Postgres into Node, just to be reduced to an integer. With a realistic dataset (a few projects of hundreds of tasks each), this is the difference between a sub-50 ms dashboard load and a multi-second one, and the payload scales with task count even though the response shape doesn't.

### Recommended fix

Replace the `tasks: true` include with a relational count and aggregate in SQL:

```ts
const memberships = await prisma.membership.findMany({
  where: { userId: user.id },
  include: {
    project: {
      include: {
        owner: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true } },
        _count: { select: { tasks: true } },
      },
    },
  },
  orderBy: { createdAt: "desc" },
});

const projects = memberships.map((m) => ({
  id: m.project.id,
  name: m.project.name,
  description: m.project.description,
  role: m.role,
  owner: m.project.owner,
  taskCount: m.project._count.tasks,
  createdAt: m.project.createdAt,
}));
```

This pushes the count down to a single grouped query and keeps the response size O(projects) instead of O(tasks).

# Code Review — TaskBoard

Top 4 issues prioritized by business impact. The first two are exploitable security bugs that should block any deploy.

---

## 1. SQL Injection in task search (`?q=`)

- **File:** [src/app/api/projects/[id]/tasks/route.ts:27-34](src/app/api/projects/%5Bid%5D/tasks/route.ts#L27-L34)
- **Category:** Security
- **Severity:** Critical

### Description

The `GET /api/projects/:id/tasks?q=…` handler builds a raw SQL string with `prisma.$queryRawUnsafe` and interpolates both the URL path's `projectId` and the user-supplied `q` parameter directly into the query. There is no escaping, parameterization, or allow-listing. Any authenticated user can read arbitrary rows from any table (including `users.password_hash`) via `UNION SELECT`, and the projectId interpolation means a future call that doesn't require membership would also be exploitable.

### Reproduction

Run with `bash` (or `zsh -c`) — interactive zsh doesn't treat `#` as a comment by default. Requires the dev server (`docker-compose up`) and `jq`.

```bash
# Log in as the lowest-privilege seeded user (a viewer)
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/auth/login \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"email":"dev@example.com","password":"password123"}' | jq -r .token)

# Grab any project id the viewer can see
PID=$(curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:3000/api/projects | jq -r '.projects[0].id')

# Exfiltrate every user's email + bcrypt hash via UNION injection in ?q=
# Payload closes the `(title ILIKE '%…%')` paren, UNIONs in a SELECT against
# `users`, and comments out the trailing OR clause + ORDER BY tail.
curl -sG -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  --data-urlencode $'q=%\') UNION SELECT id, email, email, password_hash, \'todo\'::"TaskStatus", NULL, id, 0, created_at, updated_at FROM users--' \
  "http://localhost:3000/api/projects/$PID/tasks" | jq
```

**Response (truncated):**

```json
{
  "tasks": [
    {
      "id": "cmd5...meera",
      "project_id": "meera@taskboard.dev",
      "title": "meera@taskboard.dev",
      "description": "$2a$10$XJq...redacted...bcrypt-hash",
      "status": "todo",
      "assignee_id": null,
      "created_by_id": "cmd5...meera",
      "position": 0,
      "created_at": "2026-05-13T...",
      "updated_at": "2026-05-13T..."
    },
    { "...": "one row per user, all hashes leaked" }
  ]
}
```

A `viewer` who shouldn't even be able to create tasks just walked away with every account's password hash.

### Recommended fix

Use `prisma.$queryRaw` (tagged template — parameterizes automatically) or, better, switch to the typed `findMany` API with Prisma's `contains` filter:

```ts
const tasks = await prisma.task.findMany({
  where: {
    projectId,
    OR: [
      { title:       { contains: q, mode: "insensitive" } },
      { description: { contains: q, mode: "insensitive" } },
    ],
  },
  include: { assignee: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true } } },
  orderBy: [{ status: "asc" }, { position: "asc" }],
});
```

Audit the codebase for any other `$queryRawUnsafe` usage and ban it via lint rule.

---

## 2. Broken authorization on `PATCH /api/tasks/:id` (IDOR)

- **File:** [src/app/api/tasks/[id]/route.ts:16-38](src/app/api/tasks/%5Bid%5D/route.ts#L16-L38)
- **Category:** Security
- **Severity:** Critical

### Description

The task `PATCH` handler authenticates the caller but never checks whether they are a member of the project that owns the task — compare this to the `DELETE` handler ten lines below, which does call `getProjectMembership` and `canEditTasks`. Any authenticated user can rewrite the title, description, status, assignee, or position of any task in any project simply by guessing or scraping a task id. A `viewer` (who is explicitly not allowed to edit tasks even in their own project per `canEditTasks`) can also bypass the role check this way.

### Recommended fix

Mirror the `DELETE` handler's authorization. After fetching the task, look up the caller's membership in the task's project and reject if missing or insufficient:

```ts
const existing = await prisma.task.findUnique({ where: { id } });
if (!existing) return notFound("task not found");

const membership = await getProjectMembership(user.id, existing.projectId);
if (!membership) return forbidden("you are not a member of this project");
if (!canEditTasks(membership.role)) {
  return forbidden("viewers cannot edit tasks");
}
```

Longer-term, factor this check into a small `requireTaskEditor(req, taskId)` helper so every task-mutating route uses the same path and you can't forget it on the next endpoint.

---

## 3. `User.email` has no unique constraint

- **File:** [prisma/schema.prisma:25](prisma/schema.prisma#L25)
- **Category:** Data Integrity
- **Severity:** High

### Description

The `User` model defines `email String` with no `@unique`, and both register and login use `findFirst({ where: { email } })`. The application-level check in `register/route.ts:17` is a TOCTOU race — two concurrent signups with the same email both pass the `existing` check and both insert, leaving duplicate accounts. Login then resolves a non-deterministic row, which can authenticate the wrong user (different `passwordHash`) and corrupts every downstream identity assumption (project membership, audit trails, JWT subject).

### Recommended fix

Add the constraint at the schema level and let the database enforce it:

```prisma
model User {
  // ...
  email String @unique
  // ...
}
```

Run a migration that first de-duplicates existing rows (e.g. keep the oldest `created_at` per email, reassign FKs, then drop the rest) before adding the unique index. In code, replace `findFirst` with `findUnique` and rely on a `P2002` Prisma error in the register path instead of a pre-check.

---

## 4. N+1-style over-fetch on `GET /api/projects`

- **File:** [src/app/api/projects/route.ts:10-31](src/app/api/projects/route.ts#L10-L31)
- **Category:** Performance
- **Severity:** Medium

### Description

The project list endpoint does `include: { project: { include: { tasks: true } } }` and then uses `m.project.tasks.length` purely to render a count. Every project the caller belongs to materializes every task row (title, description up to 5 KB, timestamps, foreign keys) over the wire from Postgres into Node, just to be reduced to an integer. With a realistic dataset (a few projects of hundreds of tasks each), this is the difference between a sub-50 ms dashboard load and a multi-second one, and the payload scales with task count even though the response shape doesn't.

### Recommended fix

Replace the `tasks: true` include with a relational count and aggregate in SQL:

```ts
const memberships = await prisma.membership.findMany({
  where: { userId: user.id },
  include: {
    project: {
      include: {
        owner: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true } },
        _count: { select: { tasks: true } },
      },
    },
  },
  orderBy: { createdAt: "desc" },
});

const projects = memberships.map((m) => ({
  id: m.project.id,
  name: m.project.name,
  description: m.project.description,
  role: m.role,
  owner: m.project.owner,
  taskCount: m.project._count.tasks,
  createdAt: m.project.createdAt,
}));
```

This pushes the count down to a single grouped query and keeps the response size O(projects) instead of O(tasks).

1	# Code Review — TaskBoard
2	
3	Top 4 issues prioritized by business impact. The first two are exploitable security bugs that should block any deploy.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. SQL Injection in task search (`?q=`)
8	
9	- **File:** [src/app/api/projects/[id]/tasks/route.ts:27-34](src/app/api/projects/%5Bid%5D/tasks/route.ts#L27-L34)
10	- **Category:** Security
11	- **Severity:** Critical
12	
13	### Description
14	
15	The `GET /api/projects/:id/tasks?q=…` handler builds a raw SQL string with `prisma.$queryRawUnsafe` and interpolates both the URL path's `projectId` and the user-supplied `q` parameter directly into the query. There is no escaping, parameterization, or allow-listing. Any authenticated user can read arbitrary rows from any table (including `users.password_hash`) via `UNION SELECT`, and the projectId interpolation means a future call that doesn't require membership would also be exploitable.
16	
17	### Reproduction
18	
19	Run with `bash` (or `zsh -c`) — interactive zsh doesn't treat `#` as a comment by default. Requires the dev server (`docker-compose up`) and `jq`.
20	
21	```bash
22	# Log in as the lowest-privilege seeded user (a viewer)
23	TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/auth/login \
24	  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
25	  -d '{"email":"dev@example.com","password":"password123"}' | jq -r .token)
26	
27	# Grab any project id the viewer can see
28	PID=$(curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
29	  http://localhost:3000/api/projects | jq -r '.projects[0].id')
30	
31	# Exfiltrate every user's email + bcrypt hash via UNION injection in ?q=
32	# Payload closes the `(title ILIKE '%…%')` paren, UNIONs in a SELECT against
33	# `users`, and comments out the trailing OR clause + ORDER BY tail.
34	curl -sG -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
35	  --data-urlencode $'q=%\') UNION SELECT id, email, email, password_hash, \'todo\'::"TaskStatus", NULL, id, 0, created_at, updated_at FROM users--' \
36	  "http://localhost:3000/api/projects/$PID/tasks" | jq
37	```
38	
39	**Actual response (captured 2026-05-13, truncated):**
40	
41	```json
42	{
43	  "tasks": [
44	    {
45	      "id": "cmp3kdhvw0003np61yikfjq6l",
46	      "project_id": "dev@example.com",
47	      "title": "dev@example.com",
48	      "description": "$2a$10$J5OsxJO.Eq/dkIdKpE2Egurk5OM8pkleqlm3EVx2v5F1/Gtx/rr2G",
49	      "status": "todo",
50	      "assignee_id": null,
51	      "created_by_id": "cmp3kdhvw0003np61yikfjq6l",
52	      "position": 0,
53	      "created_at": "2026-05-13T04:33:09.068Z",
54	      "updated_at": "2026-05-13T04:33:09.068Z"
55	    },
56	    {
57	      "id": "cmp3kdhvu0000np619et7fdfr",
58	      "project_id": "meera@taskboard.dev",
59	      "title": "meera@taskboard.dev",
60	      "description": "$2a$10$J5OsxJO.Eq/dkIdKpE2Egurk5OM8pkleqlm3EVx2v5F1/Gtx/rr2G",
61	      "status": "todo",
62	      ...
63	    },
64	    { "...": "3 more user rows — arjun, kavya, lina — each with bcrypt hash in `description`" },
65	    {
66	      "id": "cmp3kdhw1000pnp610mj3pn4g",
67	      "project_id": "cmp3kdhvx0006np61dgz2onud",
68	      "title": "Draft press release",
69	      "description": "Detail for: Draft press release",
70	      "status": "review",
71	      "..."
72	    }
73	  ]
74	}
75	```
76	
77	The first five entries are rows from the `users` table smuggled in via UNION — emails surfaced in `title`/`project_id`, bcrypt hashes in `description`. The remaining entries are the project's real tasks. A `viewer` (who per `canEditTasks` shouldn't even be able to create a task) just walked away with every account's password hash.
78	
79	### Recommended fix
80	
81	Use `prisma.$queryRaw` (tagged template — parameterizes automatically) or, better, switch to the typed `findMany` API with Prisma's `contains` filter:
82	
83	```ts
84	const tasks = await prisma.task.findMany({
85	  where: {
86	    projectId,
87	    OR: [
88	      { title:       { contains: q, mode: "insensitive" } },
89	      { description: { contains: q, mode: "insensitive" } },
90	    ],
91	  },
92	  include: { assignee: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true } } },
93	  orderBy: [{ status: "asc" }, { position: "asc" }],
94	});
95	```
96	
97	Audit the codebase for any other `$queryRawUnsafe` usage and ban it via lint rule.
98	
99	---
100	
101	## 2. Broken authorization on `PATCH /api/tasks/:id` (IDOR)
102	
103	- **File:** [src/app/api/tasks/[id]/route.ts:16-38](src/app/api/tasks/%5Bid%5D/route.ts#L16-L38)
104	- **Category:** Security
105	- **Severity:** Critical
106	
107	### Description
108	
109	The task `PATCH` handler authenticates the caller but never checks whether they are a member of the project that owns the task — compare this to the `DELETE` handler ten lines below, which does call `getProjectMembership` and `canEditTasks`. Any authenticated user can rewrite the title, description, status, assignee, or position of any task in any project simply by guessing or scraping a task id. A `viewer` (who is explicitly not allowed to edit tasks even in their own project per `canEditTasks`) can also bypass the role check this way.
110	
111	### Recommended fix
112	
113	Mirror the `DELETE` handler's authorization. After fetching the task, look up the caller's membership in the task's project and reject if missing or insufficient:
114	
115	```ts
116	const existing = await prisma.task.findUnique({ where: { id } });
117	if (!existing) return notFound("task not found");
118	
119	const membership = await getProjectMembership(user.id, existing.projectId);
120	if (!membership) return forbidden("you are not a member of this project");
121	if (!canEditTasks(membership.role)) {
122	  return forbidden("viewers cannot edit tasks");
123	}
124	```
125	
126	Longer-term, factor this check into a small `requireTaskEditor(req, taskId)` helper so every task-mutating route uses the same path and you can't forget it on the next endpoint.
127	
128	---
129	
130	## 3. `User.email` has no unique constraint
131	
132	- **File:** [prisma/schema.prisma:25](prisma/schema.prisma#L25)
133	- **Category:** Data Integrity
134	- **Severity:** High
135	
136	### Description
137	
138	The `User` model defines `email String` with no `@unique`, and both register and login use `findFirst({ where: { email } })`. The application-level check in `register/route.ts:17` is a TOCTOU race — two concurrent signups with the same email both pass the `existing` check and both insert, leaving duplicate accounts. Login then resolves a non-deterministic row, which can authenticate the wrong user (different `passwordHash`) and corrupts every downstream identity assumption (project membership, audit trails, JWT subject).
139	
140	### Recommended fix
141	
142	Add the constraint at the schema level and let the database enforce it:
143	
144	```prisma
145	model User {
146	  // ...
147	  email String @unique
148	  // ...
149	}
150	```
151	
152	Run a migration that first de-duplicates existing rows (e.g. keep the oldest `created_at` per email, reassign FKs, then drop the rest) before adding the unique index. In code, replace `findFirst` with `findUnique` and rely on a `P2002` Prisma error in the register path instead of a pre-check.
153	
154	---
155	
156	## 4. N+1-style over-fetch on `GET /api/projects`
157	
158	- **File:** [src/app/api/projects/route.ts:10-31](src/app/api/projects/route.ts#L10-L31)
159	- **Category:** Performance
160	- **Severity:** Medium
161	
162	### Description
163	
164	The project list endpoint does `include: { project: { include: { tasks: true } } }` and then uses `m.project.tasks.length` purely to render a count. Every project the caller belongs to materializes every task row (title, description up to 5 KB, timestamps, foreign keys) over the wire from Postgres into Node, just to be reduced to an integer. With a realistic dataset (a few projects of hundreds of tasks each), this is the difference between a sub-50 ms dashboard load and a multi-second one, and the payload scales with task count even though the response shape doesn't.
165	
166	### Recommended fix
167	
168	Replace the `tasks: true` include with a relational count and aggregate in SQL:
169	
170	```ts
171	const memberships = await prisma.membership.findMany({
172	  where: { userId: user.id },
173	  include: {
174	    project: {
175	      include: {
176	        owner: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true } },
177	        _count: { select: { tasks: true } },
178	      },
179	    },
180	  },
181	  orderBy: { createdAt: "desc" },
182	});
183	
184	const projects = memberships.map((m) => ({
185	  id: m.project.id,
186	  name: m.project.name,
187	  description: m.project.description,
188	  role: m.role,
189	  owner: m.project.owner,
190	  taskCount: m.project._count.tasks,
191	  createdAt: m.project.createdAt,
192	}));
193	```
194	
195	This pushes the count down to a single grouped query and keeps the response size O(projects) instead of O(tasks).

# Code Review — TaskBoard

Top 4 issues prioritized by business impact. The first two are exploitable security bugs that should block any deploy.

---

## 1. SQL Injection in task search (`?q=`)

- **File:** [src/app/api/projects/[id]/tasks/route.ts:27-34](src/app/api/projects/%5Bid%5D/tasks/route.ts#L27-L34)
- **Category:** Security
- **Severity:** Critical

### Description

The `GET /api/projects/:id/tasks?q=…` handler builds a raw SQL string with `prisma.$queryRawUnsafe` and interpolates both the URL path's `projectId` and the user-supplied `q` parameter directly into the query. There is no escaping, parameterization, or allow-listing. Any authenticated user can read arbitrary rows from any table (including `users.password_hash`) via `UNION SELECT`, and the projectId interpolation means a future call that doesn't require membership would also be exploitable.

### Reproduction

Run with `bash` (or `zsh -c`) — interactive zsh doesn't treat `#` as a comment by default. Requires the dev server (`docker-compose up`) and `jq`.

```bash
# Log in as the lowest-privilege seeded user (a viewer)
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/auth/login \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"email":"dev@example.com","password":"password123"}' | jq -r .token)

# Grab any project id the viewer can see
PID=$(curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:3000/api/projects | jq -r '.projects[0].id')

# Exfiltrate every user's email + bcrypt hash via UNION injection in ?q=
# Payload closes the `(title ILIKE '%…%')` paren, UNIONs in a SELECT against
# `users`, and comments out the trailing OR clause + ORDER BY tail.
curl -sG -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  --data-urlencode $'q=%\') UNION SELECT id, email, email, password_hash, \'todo\'::"TaskStatus", NULL, id, 0, created_at, updated_at FROM users--' \
  "http://localhost:3000/api/projects/$PID/tasks" | jq
```

**Actual response (captured 2026-05-13, truncated):**

```json
{
  "tasks": [
    {
      "id": "cmp3kdhvw0003np61yikfjq6l",
      "project_id": "dev@example.com",
      "title": "dev@example.com",
      "description": "$2a$10$J5OsxJO.Eq/dkIdKpE2Egurk5OM8pkleqlm3EVx2v5F1/Gtx/rr2G",
      "status": "todo",
      "assignee_id": null,
      "created_by_id": "cmp3kdhvw0003np61yikfjq6l",
      "position": 0,
      "created_at": "2026-05-13T04:33:09.068Z",
      "updated_at": "2026-05-13T04:33:09.068Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "cmp3kdhvu0000np619et7fdfr",
      "project_id": "meera@taskboard.dev",
      "title": "meera@taskboard.dev",
      "description": "$2a$10$J5OsxJO.Eq/dkIdKpE2Egurk5OM8pkleqlm3EVx2v5F1/Gtx/rr2G",
      "status": "todo",
      ...
    },
    { "...": "3 more user rows — arjun, kavya, lina — each with bcrypt hash in `description`" },
    {
      "id": "cmp3kdhw1000pnp610mj3pn4g",
      "project_id": "cmp3kdhvx0006np61dgz2onud",
      "title": "Draft press release",
      "description": "Detail for: Draft press release",
      "status": "review",
      "..."
    }
  ]
}
```

The first five entries are rows from the `users` table smuggled in via UNION — emails surfaced in `title`/`project_id`, bcrypt hashes in `description`. The remaining entries are the project's real tasks. A `viewer` (who per `canEditTasks` shouldn't even be able to create a task) just walked away with every account's password hash.

### Recommended fix

Use `prisma.$queryRaw` (tagged template — parameterizes automatically) or, better, switch to the typed `findMany` API with Prisma's `contains` filter:

```ts
const tasks = await prisma.task.findMany({
  where: {
    projectId,
    OR: [
      { title:       { contains: q, mode: "insensitive" } },
      { description: { contains: q, mode: "insensitive" } },
    ],
  },
  include: { assignee: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true } } },
  orderBy: [{ status: "asc" }, { position: "asc" }],
});
```

Audit the codebase for any other `$queryRawUnsafe` usage and ban it via lint rule.

---

## 2. Broken authorization on `PATCH /api/tasks/:id` (IDOR)

- **File:** [src/app/api/tasks/[id]/route.ts:16-38](src/app/api/tasks/%5Bid%5D/route.ts#L16-L38)
- **Category:** Security
- **Severity:** Critical

### Description

The task `PATCH` handler authenticates the caller but never checks whether they are a member of the project that owns the task — compare this to the `DELETE` handler ten lines below, which does call `getProjectMembership` and `canEditTasks`. Any authenticated user can rewrite the title, description, status, assignee, or position of any task in any project simply by guessing or scraping a task id. A `viewer` (who is explicitly not allowed to edit tasks even in their own project per `canEditTasks`) can also bypass the role check this way.

### Recommended fix

Mirror the `DELETE` handler's authorization. After fetching the task, look up the caller's membership in the task's project and reject if missing or insufficient:

```ts
const existing = await prisma.task.findUnique({ where: { id } });
if (!existing) return notFound("task not found");

const membership = await getProjectMembership(user.id, existing.projectId);
if (!membership) return forbidden("you are not a member of this project");
if (!canEditTasks(membership.role)) {
  return forbidden("viewers cannot edit tasks");
}
```

Longer-term, factor this check into a small `requireTaskEditor(req, taskId)` helper so every task-mutating route uses the same path and you can't forget it on the next endpoint.

---

## 3. `User.email` has no unique constraint

- **File:** [prisma/schema.prisma:25](prisma/schema.prisma#L25)
- **Category:** Data Integrity
- **Severity:** High

### Description

The `User` model defines `email String` with no `@unique`, and both register and login use `findFirst({ where: { email } })`. The application-level check in `register/route.ts:17` is a TOCTOU race — two concurrent signups with the same email both pass the `existing` check and both insert, leaving duplicate accounts. Login then resolves a non-deterministic row, which can authenticate the wrong user (different `passwordHash`) and corrupts every downstream identity assumption (project membership, audit trails, JWT subject).

### Recommended fix

Add the constraint at the schema level and let the database enforce it:

```prisma
model User {
  // ...
  email String @unique
  // ...
}
```

Run a migration that first de-duplicates existing rows (e.g. keep the oldest `created_at` per email, reassign FKs, then drop the rest) before adding the unique index. In code, replace `findFirst` with `findUnique` and rely on a `P2002` Prisma error in the register path instead of a pre-check.

## 4. N+1-style over-fetch on `GET /api/projects`

- **File:** [src/app/api/projects/route.ts:10-31](src/app/api/projects/route.ts#L10-L31)
- **Category:** Performance
- **Severity:** Medium

### Description

The project list endpoint does `include: { project: { include: { tasks: true } } }` and then uses `m.project.tasks.length` purely to render a count. Every project the caller belongs to materializes every task row (title, description up to 5 KB, timestamps, foreign keys) over the wire from Postgres into Node, just to be reduced to an integer. With a realistic dataset (a few projects of hundreds of tasks each), this is the difference between a sub-50 ms dashboard load and a multi-second one, and the payload scales with task count even though the response shape doesn't.

### Recommended fix

Replace the `tasks: true` include with a relational count and aggregate in SQL:

```ts
const memberships = await prisma.membership.findMany({
  where: { userId: user.id },
  include: {
    project: {
      include: {
        owner: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true } },
        _count: { select: { tasks: true } },
      },
    },
  },
  orderBy: { createdAt: "desc" },
});

const projects = memberships.map((m) => ({
  id: m.project.id,
  name: m.project.name,
  description: m.project.description,
  role: m.role,
  owner: m.project.owner,
  taskCount: m.project._count.tasks,
  createdAt: m.project.createdAt,
}));
```

This pushes the count down to a single grouped query and keeps the response size O(projects) instead of O(tasks).

# Code Review — TaskBoard

Top 4 issues prioritized by business impact. The first two are exploitable security bugs that should block any deploy.

---

## 1. SQL Injection in task search (`?q=`)

- **File:** [src/app/api/projects/[id]/tasks/route.ts:27-34](src/app/api/projects/%5Bid%5D/tasks/route.ts#L27-L34)
- **Category:** Security
- **Severity:** Critical

### Description

The `GET /api/projects/:id/tasks?q=…` handler builds a raw SQL string with `prisma.$queryRawUnsafe` and interpolates both the URL path's `projectId` and the user-supplied `q` parameter directly into the query. There is no escaping, parameterization, or allow-listing. Any authenticated user can read arbitrary rows from any table (including `users.password_hash`) via `UNION SELECT`, and the projectId interpolation means a future call that doesn't require membership would also be exploitable.

### Reproduction

Run with `bash` (or `zsh -c`) — interactive zsh doesn't treat `#` as a comment by default. Requires the dev server (`docker-compose up`) and `jq`.

```bash
# Log in as the lowest-privilege seeded user (a viewer)
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/auth/login \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"email":"dev@example.com","password":"password123"}' | jq -r .token)

# Grab any project id the viewer can see
PID=$(curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:3000/api/projects | jq -r '.projects[0].id')

# Exfiltrate every user's email + bcrypt hash via UNION injection in ?q=
# Payload closes the `(title ILIKE '%…%')` paren, UNIONs in a SELECT against
# `users`, and comments out the trailing OR clause + ORDER BY tail.
curl -sG -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  --data-urlencode $'q=%\') UNION SELECT id, email, email, password_hash, \'todo\'::"TaskStatus", NULL, id, 0, created_at, updated_at FROM users--' \
  "http://localhost:3000/api/projects/$PID/tasks" | jq
```

**Actual response (captured 2026-05-13, truncated):**

```json
{
  "tasks": [
    {
      "id": "cmp3kdhvw0003np61yikfjq6l",
      "project_id": "dev@example.com",
      "title": "dev@example.com",
      "description": "$2a$10$J5OsxJO.Eq/dkIdKpE2Egurk5OM8pkleqlm3EVx2v5F1/Gtx/rr2G",
      "status": "todo",
      "assignee_id": null,
      "created_by_id": "cmp3kdhvw0003np61yikfjq6l",
      "position": 0,
      "created_at": "2026-05-13T04:33:09.068Z",
      "updated_at": "2026-05-13T04:33:09.068Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "cmp3kdhvu0000np619et7fdfr",
      "project_id": "meera@taskboard.dev",
      "title": "meera@taskboard.dev",
      "description": "$2a$10$J5OsxJO.Eq/dkIdKpE2Egurk5OM8pkleqlm3EVx2v5F1/Gtx/rr2G",
      "status": "todo",
      ...
    },
    { "...": "3 more user rows — arjun, kavya, lina — each with bcrypt hash in `description`" },
    {
      "id": "cmp3kdhw1000pnp610mj3pn4g",
      "project_id": "cmp3kdhvx0006np61dgz2onud",
      "title": "Draft press release",
      "description": "Detail for: Draft press release",
      "status": "review",
      "..."
    }
  ]
}
```

The first five entries are rows from the `users` table smuggled in via UNION — emails surfaced in `title`/`project_id`, bcrypt hashes in `description`. The remaining entries are the project's real tasks. A `viewer` (who per `canEditTasks` shouldn't even be able to create a task) just walked away with every account's password hash.

### Recommended fix

Use `prisma.$queryRaw` (tagged template — parameterizes automatically) or, better, switch to the typed `findMany` API with Prisma's `contains` filter:

```ts
const tasks = await prisma.task.findMany({
  where: {
    projectId,
    OR: [
      { title:       { contains: q, mode: "insensitive" } },
      { description: { contains: q, mode: "insensitive" } },
    ],
  },
  include: { assignee: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true } } },
  orderBy: [{ status: "asc" }, { position: "asc" }],
});
```

Audit the codebase for any other `$queryRawUnsafe` usage and ban it via lint rule.

## 2. Broken authorization on `PATCH /api/tasks/:id` (IDOR)

- **File:** [src/app/api/tasks/[id]/route.ts:16-38](src/app/api/tasks/%5Bid%5D/route.ts#L16-L38)
- **Category:** Security
- **Severity:** Critical

### Description

The task `PATCH` handler authenticates the caller but never checks whether they are a member of the project that owns the task — compare this to the `DELETE` handler ten lines below, which does call `getProjectMembership` and `canEditTasks`. Any authenticated user can rewrite the title, description, status, assignee, or position of any task in any project simply by guessing or scraping a task id. A `viewer` (who is explicitly not allowed to edit tasks even in their own project per `canEditTasks`) can also bypass the role check this way.

### Recommended fix

Mirror the `DELETE` handler's authorization. After fetching the task, look up the caller's membership in the task's project and reject if missing or insufficient:

```ts
const existing = await prisma.task.findUnique({ where: { id } });
if (!existing) return notFound("task not found");

const membership = await getProjectMembership(user.id, existing.projectId);
if (!membership) return forbidden("you are not a member of this project");
if (!canEditTasks(membership.role)) {
  return forbidden("viewers cannot edit tasks");
}
```

Longer-term, factor this check into a small `requireTaskEditor(req, taskId)` helper so every task-mutating route uses the same path and you can't forget it on the next endpoint.

---

## 3. `User.email` has no unique constraint

- **File:** [prisma/schema.prisma:25](prisma/schema.prisma#L25)
- **Category:** Data Integrity
- **Severity:** High

### Description

The `User` model defines `email String` with no `@unique`, and both register and login use `findFirst({ where: { email } })`. The application-level check in `register/route.ts:17` is a TOCTOU race — two concurrent signups with the same email both pass the `existing` check and both insert, leaving duplicate accounts. Login then resolves a non-deterministic row, which can authenticate the wrong user (different `passwordHash`) and corrupts every downstream identity assumption (project membership, audit trails, JWT subject).

### Recommended fix

Add the constraint at the schema level and let the database enforce it:

```prisma
model User {
  // ...
  email String @unique
  // ...
}
```

Run a migration that first de-duplicates existing rows (e.g. keep the oldest `created_at` per email, reassign FKs, then drop the rest) before adding the unique index. In code, replace `findFirst` with `findUnique` and rely on a `P2002` Prisma error in the register path instead of a pre-check.

---

## 4. N+1-style over-fetch on `GET /api/projects`

- **File:** [src/app/api/projects/route.ts:10-31](src/app/api/projects/route.ts#L10-L31)
- **Category:** Performance
- **Severity:** Medium

### Description

The project list endpoint does `include: { project: { include: { tasks: true } } }` and then uses `m.project.tasks.length` purely to render a count. Every project the caller belongs to materializes every task row (title, description up to 5 KB, timestamps, foreign keys) over the wire from Postgres into Node, just to be reduced to an integer. With a realistic dataset (a few projects of hundreds of tasks each), this is the difference between a sub-50 ms dashboard load and a multi-second one, and the payload scales with task count even though the response shape doesn't.

### Recommended fix

Replace the `tasks: true` include with a relational count and aggregate in SQL:

```ts
const memberships = await prisma.membership.findMany({
  where: { userId: user.id },
  include: {
    project: {
      include: {
        owner: { select: { id: true, name: true, email: true } },
        _count: { select: { tasks: true } },
      },
    },
  },
  orderBy: { createdAt: "desc" },
});

const projects = memberships.map((m) => ({
  id: m.project.id,
  name: m.project.name,
  description: m.project.description,
  role: m.role,
  owner: m.project.owner,
  taskCount: m.project._count.tasks,
  createdAt: m.project.createdAt,
}));
```

This pushes the count down to a single grouped query and keeps the response size O(projects) instead of O(tasks).

[REVIEW.md](REVIEW.md) now opens with the full 28-bug matrix (3 Critical, 3 High, 14 Medium, 8 Low), and the original deep-dives for the top 4 stay below as worked evidence.

A few items in the matrix you may not have seen me mention before that I noticed on the second pass:

- **S3** turned out to be a third Critical, not in the original top 4: [src/app/api/projects/[id]/route.ts:25-40](src/app/api/projects/%5Bid%5D/route.ts#L25-L40) does `include: { owner: true, memberships: { include: { user: true } } }` with no `select`, so the project-detail endpoint actually returns **every member's `passwordHash`**. The types file even has `passwordHash?: string` on `ApiUser` (**A4**), which is the symptom of a leak someone already noticed and shrugged at. Easy `curl` to verify: `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" http://localhost:3000/api/projects/$PID | jq '.project.owner, .project.memberships[].user'` — you'll see `passwordHash` on every entry.
- **D3** is subtle: deleting any user that has ever created or been assigned to a task, or that owns a project, will fail with a Postgres FK error because no `onDelete` is set. Account deletion is currently impossible by design accident.
- **D5** + **D4** together mean the kanban board can end up with two tasks at the same `position` in the same column; sort order becomes whatever Postgres feels like.
- **A3** — `airtable` is in `dependencies` but no `import` references it. Either rip the dep out or finish the integration.

Happy to expand any of the matrix rows into a full write-up like 1-4 — just point me at a row number.
